items.q: What is distinctive about the ontological argument?
items.correct: It tries to prove God's existence purely from the concept of God — a priori reasoning needing no empirical evidence about the world.
items.misconception: The ontological argument uses scientific evidence to prove God exists.
items.random_wrong: The ontological argument is the same as the cosmological argument.
items.why: Anselm's argument is famously a priori — pure reasoning from definition. Students try to fit it into evidence-based frameworks, missing what makes it uniquely controversial in philosophy.

items.q: What is the doctrine of the Trinity?
items.correct: God is one being existing in three persons (Father, Son, Holy Spirit) — distinct in person but united in essence; Christians remain monotheistic.
items.misconception: Christians believe in three different gods.
items.random_wrong: The Trinity refers to heaven, earth and hell.
items.why: Students treat 'three persons' as 'three gods'. The orthodox position is one God in three distinct persons sharing one substance — a notoriously difficult concept students often misrepresent as polytheism.

items.correct: It is more accurately described as henotheistic or monistic — many gods (devas) are considered aspects or manifestations of one ultimate reality (Brahman).
items.misconception: Hinduism is straightforwardly polytheistic with millions of separate gods.
items.random_wrong: Hinduism worships no gods, only ancestors.
items.why: The 'many gods' surface obscures the underlying unity in much Hindu theology. Many traditions hold all deities to be expressions of one Brahman — students miss the monistic underpinning.

items.q: Is the ontological argument based on evidence from the world?
items.correct: No — it is a priori: it moves from the concept or definition of God (the greatest conceivable being) to God's existence, using reason alone.
items.misconception: The ontological argument starts from observations of order and purpose in the world.
items.random_wrong: The ontological argument is based on reports of miracles.
items.why: Arguments from order are teleological, not ontological. Mixing the families of argument is one of the most common and costly confusions in exam answers.

items.q: Did Aquinas accept Anselm's ontological argument?
items.correct: No — Aquinas rejected it, arguing that since we cannot know God's essence in this life, we cannot argue from God's definition to God's existence; proofs must start a posteriori from the world.
items.misconception: Aquinas endorsed the ontological argument and included it among his Five Ways.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas invented the ontological argument before Anselm.
items.why: All Five Ways are a posteriori precisely because Aquinas thought the a priori route fails for finite minds. Students assume all medieval theists endorsed each other's arguments.

items.q: Who responded to Anselm with the 'Lost Island' parody?
items.why: It's Gaunilo. “Aquinas” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which philosopher argued that 'existence is not a predicate'?
items.why: Correct answer: Immanuel Kant. “Rene Descartes” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Kant publish the Critique of Pure Reason?
items.why: The real figure is 1781. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
